evals.prompt: Use the approval-gate-design skill to help me figure out where to put a human approval step in my deploy pipeline before code ships to production.
expected_behaviors: Applies the three-clause reversibility test (unilateral undo, nothing crossed the boundary, no external commitment) to the deploy step
Identifies that a production deploy fails the 'nothing crossed the boundary' clause the instant it's live, so it needs a gate rather than a post-hoc report
Proposes a concrete digest-gated ack card (what/link/gate-owner/digest/undo-line), not just 'add a confirmation'

evals.prompt: I'm designing an approval gate for an agent that files support tickets on a customer's behalf. Walk me through your reversibility test and how to bind an approval to a digest so a stale approval can't slip through.
expected_behaviors: Explains all three clauses of the reversibility test, not just one
Explains digest binding: the ack is tied to a sha256 of the exact content, content change voids the ack, mismatched ack gets journaled rather than silently accepted
Distinguishes reversible sub-steps (e.g. drafting the ticket) from the irreversible one (the ticket reaching the customer) rather than gating the whole workflow

evals.prompt: I'm building an agent that automatically emails customers when a refund is issued. I want it to run mostly unattended, but I'm nervous about it sending a refund email to the wrong person with nobody checking. How do I structure this so it's safe to leave running overnight?
expected_behaviors: Recognizes this as an implicit request for approval-gate design even though the user never says 'approval gate'
Identifies the email send (external commitment, message reaches a human) as the irreversible step that needs the gate, not the whole pipeline
Recommends the reversible parts (drafting, computing the refund amount) can run and report without asking, per the 'act, don't ask' side of tiering
Does not recommend gating every step of the pipeline (avoids the gate-by-vibes / gate-everything anti-pattern)

evals.prompt: My script deletes stale rows from a shared database table on a schedule with nobody watching it. Someone asked me to make it 'safer' before we turn it back on. What should I change?
expected_behaviors: Applies the reversibility test to the delete (does undo require someone else's cooperation, e.g. a restore from backup another party would need to run)
Recommends a fail-closed default for the unknown/ambiguous case rather than 'probably fine to proceed'
Recommends gating only the irreversible portion (the actual delete) rather than the whole scheduled job

evals.prompt: Can you review this pull request for correctness bugs and suggest simplifications? Nothing here touches deploys or external systems, it's just a refactor of a parsing function.
expected_behaviors: Performs an ordinary code review focused on correctness and simplification

Does not introduce the reversibility test, approval gates, or digest-gated acks — this is a near-miss (code review sounds adjacent to 'review gate') but the task has no human-approval-flow design in it

evals.prompt: Help me center this div and fix the hover color on my submit button in CSS.
expected_behaviors: Does not mention approval gates, sign-off, or reversibility even though the element is literally named 'submit button' — the word 'submit' is a coincidence, not a workflow-gating question
